title: Findings (cross-skill handoff)
description: Shared JSON shape emitted by producer skills (review, standards, c4-reverse-engineer) and consumer skill reports (apply-review). The document-level 'source' field discriminates which finding subtype is used and which top-level fields are required.

schema_version.description: SemVer string, aligned with the plugin version at the time the document was written (.claude-plugin/plugin.json version). Consumers read this to identify which plugin release produced the envelope.

source.description: The producing skill, or the shape of the output for consumer skills. `update-pr` is absent deliberately — it emits review-shaped findings (with optional `pr_comment` fields) under `source: "review"`.

supplementary.description: Optional source-specific metadata that the render script uses to produce supplementary markdown. Not part of the cross-skill handoff contract; consumers may ignore it. Shape is defined by each producer skill.

runtime_scope.description: Where the affected code executes in the deployment lifecycle. Values listed from least to most severe: documentation — non-executable content (e.g., markdown, comments, READMEs). tooling — developer tooling and local scripts that never execute in CI or production. ci — CI/CD pipelines and build infrastructure; executes during integration but not at production runtime. service-internal — production runtime code not directly exposed to untrusted input. service-external — production runtime code directly exposed to external users or untrusted input.

runtime_scope_justification.description: Cite the file/module and explain its role in the deployment lifecycle.

failure_mode.description: The category of consequence if the issue manifests. Values listed from least to most severe: unclear — no specific failure scenario can be identified; the issue is stylistic, structural, or speculative. confusion — misleads developers or users (e.g., incorrect docs, misleading names, unclear APIs) without causing runtime misbehavior. build-break — prevents successful build, lint, or test execution; blocks CI but does not affect running systems. degraded-behavior — the system produces incorrect results, loses functionality, or behaves unexpectedly under specific conditions, but remains available. crash-or-outage — the system becomes unavailable (e.g., process crash, unhandled exception, resource exhaustion, denial of service). data-loss-or-security — the system's data integrity, confidentiality, or security posture is compromised (e.g., data corruption, exfiltration, unauthorized access, privilege escalation, injection, information disclosure).

failure_mode_justification.description: Describe the specific failure scenario — what goes wrong, for whom, and under what conditions.

evidence_quality.description: How strongly the finding is grounded in observable code evidence. Values listed from least to most severe: speculative — suspected based on patterns or general best practices; no concrete code path identified. inferred — a plausible scenario is visible in the code structure, but the conclusion requires assumptions about runtime state, configuration, or input that are not verified. demonstrated — a specific code path is traced from input to problematic outcome, citing concrete lines; the issue is visible from the code alone without runtime assumptions.

evidence_quality_justification.description: Summarize the evidence chain — what was traced, what was verified, and what (if anything) remains assumed.

trace_origin.description: How far back the agent traced to establish reachability. Values listed from least to most severe: local — observed at a single code site without tracing callers or callees. component — traced within a module or package boundary (e.g., function A calls function B which exhibits the issue); external reachability is not established. entry-point — traced from an identified external entry point (e.g., API handler, CLI command, CI trigger, user action) through the call chain to the problematic code.

trace_origin_justification.description: For entry-point: name the entry point and trace the path. For component: identify the module boundary. For local: explain why no caller trace was performed.

effort_to_fix.description: Estimated remediation cost. Not used in criticality assessment; used for prioritization. Values: trivial — a one-line change, rename, or config tweak with no design decisions. small — a localized fix within one file or function. moderate — changes spanning multiple files or functions, potentially requiring test updates. large — requires architectural changes, new abstractions, or significant refactoring.

effort_to_fix_justification.description: Describe the fix approach and why it requires the stated level of effort.

finding_id.description: Finding ID from the consumed Findings-*.json, e.g. C0, I3, S1.

outcome.description: applied = committed; skipped = no changes made; failed = attempted but errored; pending-commit = implemented in the working tree but left uncommitted (batch mode).

detail.description: Short free-text note (e.g. reason for skip/fail, or a human-readable summary).

commit_sha.description: Commit SHA of the change produced by this finding. Required semantics: set when outcome is 'applied'; absent for skipped/failed/pending-commit.

error_kind.description: Machine-readable error classification. Populated only when outcome is 'failed'; mirrors issue.kind so auditors can aggregate failures by cause.
